title: Dynamics of Seifert Surfaces of Torus Knots Via ECH
speaker: Morgan Weiler
abstract: Embedded contact homology (ECH) is a diffeomorphism invariant of three-manifolds due to Hutchings, defined using a contact form. This very diffeomorphism invariance makes it quite useful when studying contact dynamics, because it is possible to apply calculations using simpler contact forms to situations involving more complex ones. We will outline how a knot filtration on ECH was used in a 2015 paper of Hutchings to identify low mean action periodic orbits of disk maps, as well as several more recent generalizations due to other authors.
tags: Joint IAS/Princeton University Symplectic Geometry Seminar

proof_stages.label: Surface action problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates the goal for an exact surface symplectomorphism: find periodic orbits whose average value of the action function lies below the Calabi average, with the action function defined by df=psi^*beta-beta.

proof_stages.label: ECH and open-book dictionary
proof_stages.summary: The surface question is translated into Reeb dynamics on a contact three-manifold with an adapted open book, where Reeb action corresponds to the action function, linking with a page/binding corresponds to orbit length, and volume corresponds to the Calabi invariant.

proof_stages.label: Mapping-torus contact realization
proof_stages.summary: Hutchings's construction is recalled: build the mapping torus of the surface map, use a contact form morally of the shape lambda≈f dt+beta, and glue solid tori along the boundary, with boundary untwisting and flux becoming serious issues for more complicated pages.

proof_stages.label: Known cases and torus-knot target theorem
proof_stages.summary: After reviewing disk, annulus, and generic-surface results, the speaker states the in-progress torus-knot goal for the Seifert surface of T_{p,q}: under a boundary rotation and monodromy condition, obtain a periodic orbit with an average-action bound in terms of the Calabi invariant.

proof_stages.label: Torus-knot surfaces as tractable next topology
proof_stages.summary: The torus-knot Seifert surfaces are motivated as the next manageable step after annuli, especially through plumbed annuli and right-handed Dehn-twist monodromies, while emphasizing that contact forms create global difficulties even when contact structures look local.

proof_stages.label: Cohomological solution to the action-function hurdle
proof_stages.summary: The first hurdle is reduced to a cohomological exactness condition on psi^*beta-beta, identified with the obstruction to the open-book/Reeb-return construction, and a lemma shows how to adjust the primitive beta when psi^*-id is sufficiently surjective, with explicit torus-knot computations available.

proof_stages.label: Knot-filtration mechanism for ECH bounds
proof_stages.summary: The second hurdle is attacked by defining the ECH chain complex and the knot filtration F_B=m rot(B)+lk(alpha,B), whose filtered homology is contact-structure invariant and whose computation gives linking bounds to combine with the ECH volume theorem for action bounds.

proof_stages.label: Model contact-form computations for torus knots
proof_stages.summary: To compute the needed filtered ECH, the speaker constructs tractable model contact forms with torus-knot binding and elliptic rotation, perturbs equivariant return maps by Morse data, and explains the T_{2,3} hexagon/order-six monodromy picture that identifies the relevant generators and differential behavior.

examples.name: Hutchings's disk-map case
examples.note: The disk case is presented as the original setting where the ECH/open-book method produced a Calabi-type periodic-orbit bound, motivating the extension to more complicated pages.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker outlines Hutchings’s disk case by constructing the mapping torus/contact form, gluing in solid tori, and stating how the ECH computation pulls back to a disk-map action bound

examples.name: Two plumbed annuli with Dehn twists
examples.note: The genus-one surface obtained by plumbing two annuli is used as a motivating next test case after the annulus, with the three simple Dehn-twist combinations indicating nearby mapping-class problems.
examples.engagement_reason: the two-plumbed-annuli/right-handed-Dehn-twist case is identified with the T(2,3) trefoil page and then worked through via the hexagon model, return-map rotation, periodic points, and Morse perturbation

passes.reason: plumbing two annuli with Dehn-twist monodromies gives ordinary punctured-torus examples in an infinite mapping-class setting.
